paper_title: Beyond Language Modeling: An Exploration of Multimodal Pretraining

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: The visual world offers a critical axis for advancing foundation models beyond language. Despite growing interest in this direction, the design space for native multimodal models remains opaque. We provide empirical clarity through controlled, from-scratch pretraining experiments, isolating the factors that govern multimodal pretraining without interference from language pretraining. We adopt the Transfusion framework, using next-token prediction for language and diffusion for vision, to train on diverse data including text, video, image-text pairs, and even action-conditioned video. Our experiments yield four key insights: (i) Representation Autoencoder (RAE) provides an optimal unified visual representation by excelling at both visual understanding and generation; (ii) visual and language data are complementary and yield synergy for downstream capabilities; (iii) unified multimodal pretraining leads naturally to world modeling, with capabilities emerging from general training; and (iv) Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) enables efficient and effective multimodal scaling while naturally inducing modality specialization. Through IsoFLOP analysis, we compute scaling laws for both modalities and uncover a scaling asymmetry: vision is significantly more data-hungry than language. We demonstrate that the MoE architecture harmonizes this scaling asymmetry by providing the high model capacity required by language while accommodating the data-intensive nature of vision, paving the way for truly unified multimodal models.

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: The foundation model era has been defined largely by the success of language pretraining (OpenAI, 2022; Google, 2023). By scaling autoregressive models on trillions of text tokens, we have created systems with remarkable reasoning capabilities. Yet, fundamentally, text is human abstractiona lossy compression of reality. To borrow the allegory of Platos Cave (Plato, 375 BCE), language models have mastered the description of shadows on the wall without ever seeing the objects casting them. They capture symbols well but miss the high-fidelity physics, geometry, and causality of the physical world. Beyond this philosophical limitation lies hard, pragmatic ceiling: high-quality text data is finite and approaching exhaustion (Sutskever, 2025). In contrast, the visual world possesses an endless stream of signal outside the cave, capturing the raw dynamics of reality that language misses. As result, the path forward requires moving beyond the shadows to model the source directly. We turn to unified multimodal pretraining, treating the visual signal not as an auxiliary input, but as first-class citizen alongside language. The scientific landscape of unified multimodal pretraining remains largely opaque. While recent efforts (Wang et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2025a; Cui et al., 2025) have begun to move beyond language-only pretraining, the design space is rife with confounding variables. Rather than jointly learning from vision and language from scratch, most current methodologies (Liao et al., 2025; Deng et al., 2025) rely on initialization from pretrained language models (Grattafiori et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2025a). This paradigm prioritizes preserving existing language capabilities while adapting the model to become multimodal. Moreover, the knowledge already embedded in these pretrained backbones confounds any conclusions drawn about the multimodal training itself, making it difficult to disentangle what is learned from unified training versus what is inherited from language pretraining. Consequently, the fundamental dynamics and scaling relationships between vision and language remain poorly understood. In this work, we seek to provide empirical clarity to this landscape. We focus squarely on pretraining, as models core capabilities are largely acquired during this phase (Zhou et al., 2024). We train single model from scratch using the Transfusion framework, with next-token prediction for language and diffusion for vision, on diverse data including text, video, image-text pairs, and action-conditioned video. We conduct controlled experiments to isolate key variables and evaluate across comprehensive spectrum of tasks, ranging from language evaluation and visual understanding/generation, to planning capabilities in world modeling. Specifically, we investigate the following axes: Visual Representation: We evaluate broad spectrum of visual representations, ranging from Variational Autoencoders (VAE) and semantic representations to raw pixels. We identify Representation Autoencoders (RAE) as the optimal representation. (Section 3) Data: We study wide range of data mixtures, from pure text and video to paired image-text and action-conditioned video. We find minimal interference between modalities and even observe positive synergy in certain cases. (Section 4) World Modeling: We extend our evaluation to the Navigation World Model (NWM) setting, formatting actions directly as text tokens. We demonstrate that physical prediction capabilities emerge primarily from general multimodal pretraining (e.g. video) rather than domain-specific data. (Section 5) Architecture: We study design choices for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) in this unified multimodal setting and observe natural formation of modality separation and unification. (Section 6) Scaling Properties: We conduct IsoFLOP experiments to derive scaling laws for vision and language during unified pretraining. We uncover scaling asymmetry where vision is significantly more data-hungry than language, and find that MoE architectures effectively bridge this gap. (Section 7)

sections.title: 2 Experiment Setup
sections.content: Our goal is to study the science and design choices around unified multimodal pretraining. Across experiments, we keep the compute budget and training hyperparameters controlled within each ablation family. Unless 3 Figure 1 Overview of our study. Top: High-level model architecture. We train single autoregressive model with next-token prediction for text and next-visual state prediction with flow matching. Bottom: We study five axes: visual representations, data, world modeling, architecture, and scaling. otherwise specified, we evaluate at the end of pretraining (without any instruction tuning). We provide an overview of our training and inference protocols here, and more details in Section B.

sections.title: 2.1 Training and Inference
sections.content: Language modeling. Given text sequence (x1, . . . , xn), we minimize standard autoregressive cross-entropy: LLM = i=1 log pθ(xi x<i). (1) Image-wise flow matching for visual tokens. Let z0 denote the clean latents for an image or video frame (flattened into sequence), and let ϵ (0, I). Following flow matching formulations (Lipman et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023), we sample U[0, 1] and construct an interpolated latent zt = (1 t) ϵ + z0. The model predicts velocity field vθ(zt, t, context) and we minimize the squared error Lflow = Et,z0,ϵ [ vθ(zt, t, ) (z0 ϵ) 2 2 ] . (2) To facilitate diffusing through high-dimensional visual representations, we shift the noise schedule towards the noisier end of the spectrum (Esser et al., 2024), following RAE (Zheng et al., 2026; Tong et al., 2026). For each image/frame, single independent is sampled and applied to all tokens in that image. This independent noise injection aligns the training dynamics with Diffusion Forcing (Chen et al., 2024a). In Sections 3 and 6.1, we additionally explore alternative parameterizations such as predicting z0 directly (x-pred) (Li & He, 2025). Mixture training. Each batch contains mixture of examples drawn from several sources (text-only, videoonly, paired imagetext, and action-conditioned data). We optimize weighted combination of objectives: = λLMLLM + λflowLflow, (3) with weights chosen to stabilize joint training. Unless otherwise specified, we use λLM=1.0 and λflow=3.0. Causal vision and language masking. Training uses hybrid masking strategy implemented via FlexAttention (Dong et al., 2024a). Language modeling uses standard causal mask. In contrast, visual data uses block-wise causal mask where each video frame (or static image) is separate block. Tokens from the same frame attend bidirectionally, and also to previous tokens. This allows us to operate on any combination of sequential data from any modality. Inference. Text generation follows standard autoregressive next-token prediction. For visual generation, the model denoises using 25-step Euler sampler. The denoised latent is finally mapped back to pixel space using pretrained decoder. For the VAE encoders, we use the off-the-shelf pretrained decoders from SD-VAE (Rombach et al., 2022) and FLUX.1 (Labs, 2024). For SigLIP 2 So400m, DINOv2-L, and WebSSL-L, we use the decoders from RAE (Zheng et al., 2026; Tong et al., 2026). During training, we randomly drop the conditioning 10% of the time to enable classifier-free guidance at inference time (Ho & Salimans, 2022). We use fixed CFG of 3.0 to run all image generation evaluations and do not tune this value. 4 Figure 2 Examples of training data. We train on diverse data including text, videos, image-text, and action-conditioned video.

sections.title:
2.2 Model and Tokenization
Unified decoder-only backbone. We use a decoder-only
Transformer backbone (Vaswani et al., 2017) that is similar to
Transfusion (Zhou et al., 2025a), and train from scratch on a
mixture of vision and language data. Unlike Transfusion, we
use simple linear projection layers instead of a U-Net (Ron-
neberger et al., 2015). This design choice is motivated by
the fact that we do not adjust the number of visual tokens
for most encoders, allowing for a more direct mapping into
the transformer’s latent space. Within each self-attention
block, we use modality-specific FFNs (one for text and one
for vision tokens) instead of shared FFNs by default. We
verify this design choice in Figure 3: modality-specific FFNs
unanimously improves performance—reducing text perplexity
while improving image generation and VQA, consistent with
prior findings (Lin et al., 2024). See Section C for details.
Our default model has 2.3B total parameters and 1.5B are
activated per token due to the modality-specific FFNs; for
comparison, a standard dense model with shared FFNs would also contain 1.5B parameters. In Section 6.1,
we will go beyond modality-specific FFN layers and explore learnable modality-wise FFN routing via MoEs.
sections.content: Figure 3 Shared Modality-specific FFN. Modality separation improves multimodal learning over shared FFN. We explore MoE design in Section 6.1. Text tokens. Text is tokenized with standard BPE tokenizer from LLaMA-3 (Grattafiori et al., 2024) and trained with next-token prediction. We denote text tokens as T. Visual tokens. An image (or video frame) is mapped to latent tokens using frozen visual encoder. Our architecture is encoder-agnostic and can accept any visual input. In Section 3, we explore range of vision encoders ranging from semantic encoders (e.g., SigLIP 2 (Tschannen et al., 2025), DINOv2 (Oquab et al., 2023)) to VAEs (Kingma & Welling, 2014) to raw pixels. By default, we use SigLIP 2 So400M, unless otherwise specified. We denote images tokens as I. In this work, because we use image encoders, videos are encoded frame-by-frame. All frames are pre-processed to 224224 pixels. 5 Figure 4 RAE outperforms VAEs for both generation and understanding. RAE (SigLIP 2) achieves the best performance on DPGBench, GenEval, and VQA while maintaining text perplexity comparable to the text-only baseline.

sections.title: 2.3 Data Sources
sections.content: We use four broad categories of training data: (i) large-scale web text (DCLM (Li et al., 2024b)), (ii) raw videos (YouTube and curated video datasets (Kay et al., 2017; Goyal et al., 2017; Zellers et al., 2022)) at 1 FPS, (iii) paired imagetext data (MetaCLIP (Xu et al., 2024) and in-house Shutterstock), and (iv) action-conditioned navigation trajectories formatted as + (predicting future visual state conditioned on language action) taken from NWM (Bar et al., 2025) and annotated video data (see Section G.3 for details). We showcase some examples in Figure 2 and present more details in Section G.1.

sections.title: 3 Visual Representations for Unified Multimodal Pretraining
sections.content: It is well understood that semantic encoders perform better for understanding tasks such as VQA (Tong et al., 2024a). Yet, it has been widely assumed that VAE is necessary for visual generation (Liu et al., 2024c; Skorokhodov et al., 2025). Consequently, approaches like Janus (Wu et al., 2025; Ma et al., 2025), BAGEL (Deng et al., 2025) and others (AI et al., 2025; Fan et al., 2025b) use dual representations for generation and understanding, such as VAE and SigLIP 2 respectively. However, this dichotomy significantly complicates model design and adds overhead to both model training and inference. In contrast, recent studies on Representation Autoencoders (RAE) (Zheng et al., 2026; Tong et al., 2026) demonstrate that diffusion models can operate effectively within high-dimensional latent spaces. Leveraging these findings, we simplify our design by using single visual encoder for both understanding and generation. We study three families of encoders. First, we study VAEs from Stable Diffusion (SD-VAE) (Rombach et al., 2022) and FLUX.1 (Labs et al., 2025). Second, we study semantic encoders including both language-supervised encoders such as SigLIP 2 So400m (Tschannen et al., 2025), and self-supervised encoders such as DINOv2L (Oquab et al., 2023) and WebSSL-L (Fan et al., 2025a). We use the off-the-shelf decoder from RAE to decode latents into pixels. Finally, we study using raw pixels as direct inputs, where we patchify each image into tokens that each contain the flattened RGB values of 1414 patch. Results are shown in Figure 4. Text performance. We observe that all visual representations achieve similar (and sometimes even slightly better) text perplexity compared to the text-only baseline, with raw pixels performing the best. However, the marginal difference indicates that multimodal pretraining does not significantly affect the models language capabilities compared to training on text alone, regardless of the visual representation. Visual generation and understanding. Semantic encoders consistently outperform VAE-based encoders on both visual understanding and generation. For example, SigLIP 2 not only outperforms FLUX.1 on Figure 5 Visual data does not compete with text. Text + Video matches the text-only PPL on DCLM, suggesting that raw visual data is compatible with language modeling. Figure 6 I/T data enables visual capabilities. I/T pairs are essential for visual understanding and generation. Text + Video contains no I/T data, so image generation is omitted. VQA, but also on image generation benchmarks such as DPGBench and GenEval. This echoes findings from RAE (Zheng et al., 2026; Tong et al., 2026) that high-dimensional visual representations are as effective as, if not more effective than, low-dimensional VAE latents for generation. It shows that single encoder suffices for both visual understanding and generation. Thus, we adopt SigLIP 2 as the default vision encoder. We also evaluate training directly on raw pixels, with optionally the x-prediction parameterization from JiT (Li & He, 2025). We observe that while using raw pixels underperforms semantic encoders in generation quality, the performance gap in VQA accuracy is comparatively narrow. Given this competitive understanding performance, we believe that learning directly from raw pixels remains promising direction for future research, particularly with more compute and scale. Suggestion 1: single RAE-based encoder (e.g. SigLIP 2) simplifies the architecture by excelling at both visual understanding and generation, while preserving text performance.

sections.title: 4 Understanding the Impact of Data
sections.content: We study the impact of pretraining data composition. Our unified framework (Section 2) is capable of modeling any combination of multimodal data, such as text, video, image-text pairs (I/T), and action-conditioned video. We first compare text-only pretraining against multimodal pretraining (Section 4.1), then dissect the role of text distribution in I/T data (Section 4.2), and finally demonstrate positive transfer from multimodal co-training (Section 4.3).

sections.title: 4.1 Pretraining Data Composition
sections.content: The premise of unified multimodal pretraining is to leverage all available data. However, it is unclear how each data type contributes to or interferes with the final model. To better understand this, we study three representative data mixtures (see Section G.1 for details): 1) Text + Video (raw video without text annotations), 2) Text + MetaCLIP (I/T pairs) and 3) Text + Video + MetaCLIP + Action (all the above + action-conditioned videos). All multimodal models are trained on 1T tokens (520B text + 520B multimodal) and are compared to text-only baseline trained on 520B text tokens. In Figure 5, we find that Text + Video achieves the Vision data minimally impacts text performance. best perplexity of all data mixtures on both the DCLM val set and the in-house Notes corpus. On DCLM, Text + Video even outperforms the text-only baseline, suggesting that video data is at least compatible with, and possibly beneficial for language modeling. This also suggests that vision itself is not the primary cause of modality competition. On the other hand, Text + MetaCLIP achieves the worst perplexity of all data mixtures. Text + Video + MetaCLIP + Actions only slightly degrades compared to the text-only baseline, suggesting that video + action trajectories are also complementary to text. We hypothesize that text degradation stems from shift in the text distribution from introducing image captions, and further investigate this in Section 4.2. Second, we observe that across all data mixtures, perplexity relative to the text-only baseline degrades on the more out-of-distribution Notes corpus, but the relative trends remain the same. This suggests that multimodal pretraining may introduce minor trade-off in text generalization. 7 Figure 7 I/T data sources exhibit complementary trade-offs. Each source of I/T data has distinct strengths across text perplexity, generation, and VQA. Combining MetaCLIP (I2T) with Shutterstock (T2I) captures the strengths of both, suggesting that strategically using different I/T sources can outperform using single source of I/T data. I/T data enables visual generation and understanding. Consistent with prior work (Tong et al., 2024a; Zhu et al., 2025), in Figure 6, we find that I/T data is essential for acquiring understanding and generation capabilities. Further, our results on VQA demonstrate that understanding tasks benefit significantly from broader data diversity, with the full data mixture surpassing all other combinations of data.

sections.title:
4.2 Image-Text Distribution
In Section 4.1, we observed that text perplexity slightly degrades when I/T data is introduced (e.g. Text
+ MetaCLIP). We hypothesize this stems from the distributional gap between pretraining text and image
captions. We study three types of image-text data sources: web-crawled data (MetaCLIP), synthetic captions
(MetaCLIP Recaption), and high-aesthetic images (Shutterstock/SSTK).
sections.content: As quantified in Table 1, synthetic recaptions and in-house data exhibit significantly larger cosine distance from the pretraining corpus (DCLM) than standard MetaCLIP captions, directly correlating with the observed degradation in language perplexity, especially on Notes (Figure 7). MetaCLIP MetaCLIP (Recap) SSTK Cosine Distance () 0.196 0. 0.215 Table 1 Cosine distance of image captions to DCLM. See Section G.4 for details. Different I/T sources affect visual understanding and generation differently: recaptioned data improves VQA, while high-aesthetic data (SSTK) improves generation quality (Figure 7). Consequently, we decouple the data by objective, using MetaCLIP for image-to-text and SSTK for text-to-image. This captures the strengths of both across all metrics, and suggests that data can be independently chosen to target specific capabilities.

sections.title: 4.3 Synergy in Multimodal Pretraining
sections.content: We observed in Section 4.1 that adding diverse multimodal data in equal proportion to text minimally impacts text performance. We now explore whether multimodal pretraining can actively benefit any capabilities. Vision and language are complementary. We measure how each modality affects the other by training models across all combinations of {0, 25, 50, 75, 100}B text and multimodal tokens (Figure 8). Adding text tokens to fixed vision budget consistently improves diffusion loss and GenEval score, clearly surpassing the multimodal-only baseline. We also measure text performance (Section D.3) and confirm that vision minimally impacts language, while language helps vision. This benefit likely arises because image generation benchmarks (e.g. GenEval) are text-conditioned, so improved language modeling improves text-to-image alignment. Synergy extends to visual understanding. Motivated by this, we ask whether the same synergy extends to visual understanding, or if VQA performance primarily requires domain-specific data. We train models on 20B VQA tokens supplemented with 80B of heterogeneous data (video, MetaCLIP, or text), and compare against both 20B VQA-only baseline and 100B VQA-only baseline. As shown in Figure 9, all mixed variants outperform even the 100B baseline, despite using 5 less in-domain data. This shows that simply scaling task-specific data is inferior to diverse pretraining, which provides stronger foundation. The benefit of 8 Figure 8 Multimodal co-training exceeds unimodal performance. Across all token budgets, adding text tokens to fixed vision budget consistently improves generation qual- ), surpassing the multimodal-only baseline ( ity ( ). Figure 9 General pretraining outperforms specialized scaling. Supplementing 20B VQA tokens with general-purpose data (text, video, or image-text) yields higher accuracy than training on 100B VQA tokens alone. language data aligns with prior findings (Han et al., 2026), and notably we also observe that even out-of-domain data such as unlabeled video improves VQA performance. We further test this at scale by comparing pretraining on 520B text + 520B multimodal tokens (video, MetaCLIP, action) against text-only baseline, then finetuning both on VQA following Tong et al. (2024a) (see Section E.6). As shown in Figure 10, multimodal pretraining improves VQA over text-only pretraining across variety of visual representations. Notably, semantic encoders such as SigLIP 2 consistently outperform VAE-based encoders, reinforcing our findings in Section 3. Figure 10 Multimodal pretraining is better for VQA. Multimodal pretraining (dark bars) consistently outperforms text-only baselines (light bars) after finetuning on VQA. Suggestion 2: Train with multimodal data (e.g. video, image-text). Visual data does not degrade language modeling, and diverse pretraining yields synergy for downstream tasks such as world modeling and VQA.

sections.title: 5 Towards World Modeling in Unified Multimodal Models
sections.content: Motivated by the observation that language and vision are complementary, and that multimodal pretraining benefits VQA, we then explore whether multimodal models can be extended to do world modeling (Nguyen & Widrow, 1989; Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; LeCun, 2022), without any architectural changes. We adopt the setting of Navigation World Model (NWM) (Bar et al., 2025) where the task is to predict the next visual state, conditioned on given context state and navigation action (Statet + Action Statet+1). However, unlike NWM which encodes navigation actions (translation and rotation deltas) as specialized continuous vectors, we directly represent actions as standard text tokens (i.e. numerical strings). This allows 9 Figure 11 Navigation actions are encoded directly as text tokens. We utilize data from Navigation World Model (NWM), where training sequence consists of four context frames and navigation action formatted as natural language text. The model is trained to predict the resulting target view. Figure 12 Unsupervised video data drives world modeling performance. Supplementing the NWM baseline with generalpurpose video data yields the best performance. Figure 13 World modeling transfers with minimal alignment. Performance saturates at 1%, suggesting that the core capability is mostly acquired from general pretraining. us to frame the task as + prediction within our unified multimodal model. See Figure 11 for an example. Unlike NWM, we do not introduce any action-specific adapters nor modify the architecture.

sections.title: 5.1 NWM Data and Protocol
sections.content: We follow the data and evaluation protocols established by NWM (Bar et al., 2025). The model is trained on egocentric robot navigation datasets (e.g. SCAND, RECON) (Shah et al., 2021; Karnan et al., 2022), where each sample consists of 4 context frames, navigation action, and the resultant target frame. For evaluation, we perform zero-shot planning using the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) (Rubinstein, 1997) with = 120 samples over an 8-step horizon, optimizing action sequences to minimize the LPIPS distance (Zhang et al., 2018) between the predicted final frame and the goal image, and report the absolute trajectory error (ATE) and relative pose error (RPE). See details in Section F.1.

sections.title: 5.2 World Modeling Emerges from Multimodal Pretraining
sections.content: Does effective world modeling stem primarily from domain-specific navigation data, or does it emerge from broader multimodal capabilities? To test this, we compare multimodal models trained on 50B NWM tokens and 50B multimodal data (text, MetaCLIP, videos with text annotations, or video), against baseline trained on 50B tokens of only NWM data. The results in Figure 12 mirror the VQA results in Figure 9. Scaling domain-specific NWM data from 50B to 100B tokens yields moderate improvements in ATE and RPE, but multimodal pretraining provides better results. Specifically, adding pure video yields the biggest gain, but all other modalities including MetaCLIP and text also help. This suggests that world modeling relies more on capabilities acquired from multimodal pretraining, rather than domain-specific data. This mirrors findings from earlier work (Hafner et al., 2025). World modeling transfers from general training. To further disentangle the origin of world modeling capabilities, we conduct an ablation study varying the ratio of NWM data while keeping the total training budget fixed at 200B tokens (see Section F.1 for details). As shown in Figure 13, performance saturates 10 Figure 14 Zero-shot model controllability via natural language. Given four context frames (ctx0ctx3), the model predicts eight future frames (p1-p8) conditioned on WASD-style or natural language navigation actions. rapidly with respect to domain data volume. We observe that the model reaches competitive performance with as little as 1% in-domain data, with negligible gains observed at higher ratios. Collectively, our findings reinforce our hypothesis that abilities such as navigation and VQA are primarily learned from general pretraining and only require minimal in-domain data.

sections.title: 5.3 Qualitative Rollouts with Free-form Language as Actions
sections.content: We visualize the models rollout capabilities by providing four context frames and predicting subsequent frames autoregressively. We first predefine four actions corresponding to standard WASD controls (Forward , Left , Backward , Right ); we refer readers to Section F.1 for the specific numerical values of these actions. As consequence of our multimodal pretraining, we can also condition on free-form natural language prompts as actions (e.g. get out of the shadow!), which are out-of-distribution. See Section F.2 for more qualitative examples. Figure 14 displays generated sequence produced by controlling the model with these inputs. We observe that the generated frames maintain visual consistency while faithfully executing the commanded actions. The model is also able to generate trajectories directly based on natural language commands. This language-driven navigation emerges as zero-shot capability, demonstrating how semantic understanding successfully transfers from the broader multimodal pretraining. Suggestion 3: Unified multimodal pretraining unlocks world modeling. Represent actions as text without architectural changes; capabilities emerge via general training with minimal domain-specific data. 11 Figure 15 Fine-grained experts improve performance; x-pred benefits high-dimensional diffusion. Increasing granularity improves all metrics up to G=16. For RAE (SigLIP 2), x-pred (solid) outperforms v-pred (dashed) on image generation; for VAE (FLUX.1), v-pred is better.

sections.title: 6 Unified Multimodal Architecture Design
sections.content: We show in Figure 3 that simply replacing shared FFNs with modality-specific FFNs is highly effective, demonstrating the potential of modest capacity separation. However, modality-specific FFNs separate capacity between two modalities evenly, which may not reflect the ideal capacity allocation. We now explore whether Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) can learn this separation dynamically by decoupling total capacity from active compute. We study the MoE design space (Section 6.1), the specialization patterns that emerge (Section 6.2), and alternative design choices (Section 6.3).

sections.title: 6.1 Exploring MoEs for Unified Multimodal Models
sections.content: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) generalizes fixed modality-specific FFNs by learning dynamic decomposition of the FFN layers, routing each token to subset of specialized experts (Shazeer et al., 2017; Fedus et al., 2022). Recent work (Liu et al., 2024a,b; OpenAI, 2025; Team et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2025a) has demonstrated the scalability of MoE for language modeling. Here, we study its design space for unified multimodal pretraining. Unless otherwise noted, all MoE experiments are trained for 57B tokens on 50/50 mixture of DCLM text and Shutterstock image data. Granularity. We first analyze the role of expert granularity G, which we define relative to model dimension dmodel and expert dimension dexpert as = 4dmodel dexpert . (4) We sweep {1, 4, 16, 32, 64} at fixed active compute: as increases, the number of active experts increases while the dimension of each expert shrinks proportionally. G=1 corresponds to baseline of 16 large experts (d=8192) with Top-1 routing, while G=64 yields 1024 small experts (d=128)1 with Top-64 routing. We test two visual representationsRAE (SigLIP 2) and VAE (FLUX.1)to understand how the visual latent space affects MoE efficacy. We also test two diffusion prediction targets: x-pred (Li & He, 2025) and v-pred (Lipman et al., 2023). Because finer-grained experts have smaller dimension which may fall below the rank of the visual latent space, we hypothesize that x-pred can mitigate this by leveraging the manifold assumption. All models have 13.5B total parameters (1.5B active); see Section E.7 for detailed configurations. As shown in Figure 15, higher granularity is critical: increasing from 1 to 16 substantially improves both language and vision modeling, as many smaller experts enable better capacity allocation across modalities. Notably, the two modalities saturate at different granularitiesvision at = 4, language at = 16suggesting that language benefits more from fine-grained routing. Gains plateau beyond = 32, so we adopt = 16 for subsequent experiments. 1Due to implementation constraints in TorchTitan (Liang et al., 2025), we use 1008 experts for the = 64 setting. 12 Figure 16 Sparsity scales multimodal performance. At fixed active compute (16 experts), increasing total expert count from 32 to 1008 (and thus total parameter count) consistently improves both language (lower PPL) and vision (lower diffusion loss, higher GenEval score). Figure 17 Semantic representations scale better with sparsity. We fix 16 active experts and increase the total expert count. Text loss improves consistently regardless of the vision encoder, but diffusion loss saturates for VAE (FLUX.1) while continuing to improve for RAE (SigLIP 2). Prediction target depends on visual representation. The optimal diffusion prediction target varies with the visual representation (Figure 15, solid vs. dashed). For RAE (SigLIP 2), x-pred (Li & He, 2025) consistently outperforms v-pred on image generation across all granularities, with minimal impact on language. For VAE (FLUX.1), however, x-pred causes text perplexity to spike as granularity increases, while v-pred scales cleanly. This suggests that x-pred is less stable for low-dimensional representations as the expert dimension shrinks, while high-dimensional representations can better leverage higher expert granularity. Sparsity. MoE decouples total capacity from active compute; the number of active experts is fixed while the total expert pool grows, thus efficiently increasing model capacity without additional training or inference cost. Using the optimal = 16 from the granularity sweep, we fix the active budget (16 experts, hidden dim 512) and increase the total expert count from 32 to 1008, reducing the active ratio from 50% to 1.6%. We also compare against 1.5B dense baseline with the same compute budget. As shown in Figure 16, both modalities benefit consistently from increased sparsity: text PPL decreases and visual generation improves as total experts grow from 32 to over 1000all at fixed compute. This confirms that MoE, which is already widely used for language, can also extend to unified multimodal pretraining. The training loss (Figure 17) reveals further distinction between visual representations: for RAE (SigLIP 2), both text and diffusion loss continue to improve with more experts, while for VAE (FLUX.1), diffusion loss saturates, suggesting that semantic representations benefit more from sparsity. Expert design choices. Given that language and vision respond differently to granularity, we explore whether modality-specific expert designs help. Previous work (Dai et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024a) in language modeling shows that designating certain experts as always active (shared experts) improves performance. We compare three configurations, all with 256 total experts and 16 active: 1. No Shared Expert: Standard routing where the top-16 experts are selected from the full pool of 256. 13 Figure 18 Emergent Modality Specialization. Expert specialization forms naturally. Most experts are text-focused, but later layers contain more vision and multimodal experts. Figure 19 Experts do not specialize in diffusion timesteps. CV scores are distributed near 0.15, indicating vision experts are time-invariant. 2. Global Shared Expert: 1 expert is designated as shared (always active), and the router selects the top-15 from the remaining 255. 3. Per-Modality Shared Expert: We designate 1 shared expert for text and 1 for vision. For any given token, only the corresponding modality-specific shared expert is activated, along with the top-15 routed from the remaining 254. As shown in Table 2, while global shared expert improves over the baseline, per-modality shared experts yield the best performance across all metrics. This suggests that different modalities have distinct capacity needs that benefit from dedicated computationa theme we examine in depth in Section 6.2.

sections.title: 6.2 Emergent Expert Specialization
sections.content: In Section 6.1, we explored the MoE design space across granularity, sparsity, prediction targets, and shared experts, establishing that MoE is effective for multimodal pretraining. We now ask: what does the model learn? Unlike fixed modality-specific FFNs (Section 2), MoE imposes no human priors on how capacity is allocated between modalities. We analyze 13.5B-parameter MoE model (1.5B active, G=16, 256 experts, SigLIP 2, x-pred) trained on 1T mixed multimodal tokens, examining expert allocation along three dimensions: modality, diffusion timesteps, and visual tasks (see Section E.8 for methodology). Modality specialization emerges naturally. We classify each expert as Text, Vision, or Multimodal based on its routing preference across held-out DCLM (text) and CC12M (image) tokens. As shown in Figure 18, the model allocates significantly more experts to text than to vision, despite the use of an auxiliary load-balancing loss that encourages uniform utilization. This emergent asymmetry is consistent with the scaling laws we later derive in Section 7 and mirrors recent findings (Wang et al., 2026): language is parameter-hungry while vision is data-hungry, so the model naturally devotes more dedicated capacity to language via MoE. We also observe that expert composition evolves across depth: early layers are dominated by text-specific experts, while later layers contain progressively more vision and multimodal experts (Figure 18). This suggests that the model learns separate-then-integrate processing strategy of first processing each modality with dedicated parameters and later fusing them in deeper layers. 14 Figure 20 Visual generation and understanding share the same experts. We compare the expert selection rates for image generation (x-axis) versus image understanding (y-axis) across different network depths. We observe very high Pearson correlation ( = 0.90) across all layers. Vision experts are general-purpose. natural hypothesis is that vision experts might specialize across the diffusion noise schedule, separating high-noise denoising from fine-grained refinement, as explicitly enforced in architectures like Wan (Wan et al., 2025). We test this by measuring the Coefficient of Variation (CV) of expert selection rates across 10 timestep bins (see Section E.8). As shown in Figure 19, CV scores cluster near 0.15, indicating minimal timestep specializationthe same experts process the same visual tokens throughout the entire generation trajectory. Notably, visual understanding and generation share similar set of experts. Comparing routing patterns for image-to-text (understanding) versus text-to-image (generation), we observe very high correlation (r 0.90) across all layers (Figure 20): the experts activated for captioning are the same experts activated for denoising. This suggests that with learnable capacity separation, the model is able to converge to truly unified visual representation for both tasksconsistent with prior work showing that understanding and generation are mutually beneficial (Tong et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025c; Yue et al., 2026).

sections.title: 6.3 Stacking Design Choices
sections.content: The preceding sections studied each design axis in isolation: visual representation (Section 3), data (Section 4), and MoE configuration (Section 6.1). We now stack these choices progressively to verify these changes are complementary, building from Transfusion baseline toward our final configuration. We also compare against alternative approaches such as Mixture of Transformers (MoT) (Liang et al., 2024). All models are trained with identical hyperparameters, data, token counts, and FLOPs. Figure 21 summarizes the results. FFN structure. Replacing the shared FFN with modality-specific FFNs (separate weights for text and vision), as in LMFusion (Shi et al., 2024), improves perplexity from 15.93 to 15.13 and DPG score from 0.45 to 0.47, consistent with Figure 3. Even this simplest form of capacity separation reduces modality competition at no additional inference cost, since only one FFN is active per token (see Section C). We adopt modality-specific FFNs as the default for subsequent comparisons, until we explore MoE. Vision encoder. Using modality-specific FFN, we compare four vision encoders: three single-encoder configurations (SD-VAE, FLUX.1, and SigLIP 2 for RAE-style diffusion) and dual-encoder setup that uses SigLIP 2 for understanding and SD-VAE for generation, as in Bagel (Deng et al., 2025) and Janus (Ma et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025d). SigLIP 2 significantly outperforms all alternatives, improving DPG score from 0.47 to 0.57 while also reducing perplexity from 15.13 to 15.06. Notably, the dual-encoder baseline falls short of the single SigLIP 2 encoder on both metrics, suggesting that unified semantic representation is more effective than separate encoding pathways. These results are consistent with Section 3 and Section 7. Parameter separation strategy. Using SigLIP 2 and modality-specific FFN, we compare three parameter separation approaches: dense (no further separation), deep separation via MoT (Liang et al., 2024; Deng et al., 2025), and data-driven separation via MoE. MoE achieves the best trade-off, reaching the lowest perplexity (12.49) and highest DPG score (0.63), outperforming both the dense model and MoT. Beyond raw performance, MoE has structural advantage: capacity allocation between modalities is difficult to determine priori. Fixed separation cannot adapt to different inputs, whereas MoE learns to route Figure 22 Semantic encoders and MoE capture more world knowledge. WISE scores across six knowledge categories and overall. Models with semantic encoders (MoE, MoT, SigLIP 2) consistently outperform VAE-based models (SD-VAE, FLUX.1) by 34, with MoE achieving the highest overall score. each token independently during training, adapting capacity allocation to the input (Section 6.1). This echoes the bitter lesson (Sutton, 2019): learning from data generally outperforms hand-crafted designs. Our conclusions stem from from-scratch pretraining setting. Approaches such as MoT have proven effective when finetuning pretrained dense LLMs (Liang et al., 2024; Deng et al., 2025). However, as language models increasingly adopt native MoE architectures (Liu et al., 2024b; Team et al., 2025), extending their routing networks with vision and multimodal experts offers natural path to multimodal finetuning. Prediction target. Finally, we compare v-prediction and x-prediction objectives within the best architecture (MoE + SigLIP 2). Switching from v-pred to x-pred further improves DPG score from 0.63 to 0.65, consistent with the analysis in Sections 3 and 6.1. Knowledge-informed generation. While standard metrics like FID evaluate distributional visual quality, key promise of unified models is their ability to transfer world knowledge from language into visual generation. To test how our architectural design choices affect this capability, we evaluate the models on the WISE benchmark (Niu et al., 2025), which probes factual understanding across categories such as science and culture. Figure 22 reveals two findings. First, the choice of vision encoder is critical for semantic generation: semantic encoders (e.g., SigLIP 2) outperform VAE-based models (SD-VAE, FLUX.1, and dual-encoder) by 34 across all knowledge categories, regardless of the separation strategy. Second, for fixed encoder, data-driven sparsity is greatly beneficial: MoE outperforms both MoT and the dense baseline, suggesting that learned routing better transfers semantic priors for generation. Figure 21 Modality-specific FFN, SigLIP 2, MoE, and x-pred achieve best unified performance. Starting from Transfusion baseline, we progressively stack the best design choices for FFN, vision encoder, capacity separation, and prediction target. Each row shows Perplexity () and DPG score (). 16 Figure 23 Scaling laws for unified dense models. We compute IsoFLOP curves across compute budgets. We project the optimal model size and token count for each FLOP budget, and find that the vision modality is more data-hungry, while text follows Chinchilla-like scaling. Figure 24 Scaling laws and efficiency benchmarks for unified dense models. We fit L(C) = Dense Multimodal outperforms both the unimodal Text-Only and T2I-Only baselines across all four metrics. α + to each model class. Suggestion 4: Use MoE in unified models: it outperforms hand-crafted separation strategies and naturally learns modality-specific specialization from the data.

sections.title: 7 Scaling Laws for Unified Multimodal Models
sections.content: Prior scaling studies primarily examine unimodal language models (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022; Tian et al., 2026). Shukor et al. (2025) studies scaling for multimodal models that only output text. We derive scaling laws for both vision and language, and investigate how architecture influences these trends. IsoFLOP methodology. Following Chinchilla-style analyses (Hoffmann et al., 2022), we sweep model sizes and token counts at fixed compute budgets C. We estimate FLOPs via torchtitan (Liang et al., 2025), applying 6N for dense models and using active parameters (Nactive) for MoE, since each token routes to subset of experts. We measure validation loss on held-out data (DCLM for language, CC12M for vision) and fit parabola in log-parameter space to estimate Nopt(C). We then fit linear regression between log Nopt and log C, assuming the power-law relationship Nopt and Dopt where + = 1. Compute-optimal for dense models. Figure 23 presents dense IsoFLOP results. Fitting power laws Nopt and Dopt reveals divergent scaling between modalities. For Language, 0.47 and 0.53, which is nearly balanced and consistent with Chinchilla scaling laws. For Vision, 0.37 and 17 Figure 25 Scaling laws for multimodal MoE models. MoE narrows the vision-language exponent gap from 0.10 (dense) to 0.05 (Dopt 0.64 for vision vs. 0.59 for language). Figure 26 Scaling laws and efficiency benchmarks for unified MoE models. MoE Multimodal closely tracks unimodal MoE baselines across all four metrics, with near-identical FID and competitive language perplexity at 1021 FLOPs. b/a opt 0.63, indicating that vision is more data-hungry. These distinct exponents show that no single computeoptimal trend governs unified models. This asymmetry creates practical dilemma at scale: the data demands of both modalities cannot be simultaneously satisfied. Relating optimal tokens directly to model size (Dopt ). From 1B parameter baseline, this relative demand increases by 14 at 100B parameters and 51 at 1T. Although recent language models (Grattafiori et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024b) are often heavily overtrained, this widening gap forces compromise: either under-train the vision modality or expend excessive compute over-training language. At the 1T parameter scale, satisfying visions optimal data requirement would demand compute and data volumes beyond what is currently used for language pretraining. ), the ratio of required vision data to language data grows as O(N 0.57 Compute efficiency for dense models. We compare Dense Multimodal with RAE (SigLIP 2) against two unimodal baselines: Text-Only and T2I-Only. To characterize scaling near the entropy floor, we fit α + (Kaplan et al., 2020), where is irreducible loss, selecting via grid search to minimize L(C) = log-space MSE. Figure 24 shows that the unified model matches or exceeds unimodal baselines. For Language, Dense Multimodal matches the Text-Only baseline across the entire compute range, reaching Notes PPL 23.7 vs. 23.8 and DCLM PPL 13.3 vs. 13.6 at 1021 FLOPs. For Vision, Dense Multimodal outperforms T2I-Only (DPG 0.622 vs. 0.598; FID 39.3 vs. 41.5), suggesting that joint language training provides complementary signal that improves generation quality. Figure 25 presents the MoE IsoFLOP results. Under this sparse regime, Language exhibits 0.41, 0.59, while 18 Vision exhibits 0.36, 0.64. Moving to MoE reduces the scaling asymmetry: the language data exponent increases from 0.53 to 0.59, moving closer to visions data-intensive regime. Because both modalities benefit from higher sparsity, further increasing the total expert count provides mechanism to further close this gap. Sparsity thus serves as practical architectural lever to balance the divergent data demands of unified models. MoE compute efficiency. Figure 26 compares MoE Multimodal with RAE (SigLIP 2) against unimodal MoE baselines across the full compute range. Across all four metrics, the unified model closely tracks its unimodal counterparts. At 1021 FLOPs, it reaches DCLM PPL 12.3 vs. 12.0 for MoE Text-Only, and FID 39.2 vs. 39.8 for MoE T2I-Only. MoE enables single model to match unimodal performance on both modalities with minimal overhead, effectively leveraging sparse expert capacity.

sections.title: 8 Related Work
sections.content: Multimodal pretraining. Multimodal pretraining involves training models from scratch on mixed-modal data, and is distinct from finetuning pretrained backbones. Multimodal pretraining can be divided into understanding-only and unified understanding and generation approaches. Understanding-only models (Li et al., 2024a; Shukor et al., 2025) output only text, using visual data to condition language modeling. Unified methods output both text and images: early work quantized images into discrete tokens (Oord et al., 2017; Razavi et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2022) for next-token prediction (Lu et al., 2022a; Aghajanyan et al., 2022; Team, 2024; Lu et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024), while recent work (Zhou et al., 2025a; Cui et al., 2025) incorporates diffusion. We build on Transfusion and systematically study the design spacevisual representation, architecture, and datafor unified visual understanding and generation. Unified models and their architectures. Unified models (Zhang et al., 2025a; Xiao et al., 2025; Geng et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2025a; Xin et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025a; Wang et al., 2025b; Wei et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025a; Li et al., 2025d; Nguyen et al., 2025; Pan et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025b; Zhang et al., 2026) input and output multimodal datatypically vision and language. One line of work (Dai et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2024a; Ge et al., 2024; Dong et al., 2024b; Tong et al., 2025; Pan et al., 2025) connects pretrained language models (Touvron et al., 2023; Grattafiori et al., 2024) to pretrained diffusion models (Rombach et al., 2022; Podell & et al., 2024; Labs, 2024; Xie et al., 2025a), leveraging the LLM to provide conditioning for the diffusion model, typically via adapter training or end-to-end finetuning. More recent works (Liang et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2024; Ma et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025d; Liao et al., 2025; Xie et al., 2025b; Deng et al., 2025; Xie et al., 2025c) train diffusion models from scratch jointly with the language model. These works incorporate different architectural designs, varying levels of separation between language and diffusion models. Transfusion (Zhou et al., 2025a) and the Janus series (Wu et al., 2025; Ma et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025d) leverage single transformer to handle both language and vision modeling, while other recent approaches introduce stricter separation, such as splitting FFNs (Shi et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2024) or attention blocks (Liang et al., 2024; Deng et al., 2025). Recent studies (OpenAI, 2024; Tong et al., 2025; Liao et al., 2025) have shown that unification offers advantages over training T2I models alone, enabling models to leverage richer textual priors for visual generationunified models outperform specialized generation-only models on benchmarks (Niu et al., 2025; Li et al., 2026; Yang et al., 2026a) like WISE. We compare these architectures systematically in Section 6.3 and demonstrate that instead of manually designing modality separation, we can employ MoE architectures to learn the optimal degree of separation from data. Visual representations. Understanding and generation have historically used separate representations. Understanding relies on high-dimensional semantic latents from language-supervised (Radford et al., 2021; Zhai et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2023, 2024b; Chen et al., 2024b; Xu et al., 2024; Tschannen et al., 2025) or self-supervised (Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2021, 2020; Caron et al., 2021; Fan et al., 2023; Oquab et al., 2023; Fan et al., 2025a; Xu et al., 2025b) models. Generation favors low-dimensional VAE latents (Kingma & Welling, 2014; Rombach et al., 2022; Labs, 2024; Cao et al., 2025). Recent work (Chen et al., 2025c,a; Zheng et al., 2026; Tong et al., 2026) shows semantic latents also enable strong generation. We demonstrate that VAE latents degrade visual understanding (Figure 10), while semantic latents with RAE excel at both tasks. World models. World models (Nguyen & Widrow, 1989; Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; LeCun, 2022) enable testtime reasoning for embodied tasks. This paradigm evolved from in-domain models for policy training (Hafner 19 et al., 2020, 2021; Hansen et al., 2022; Robine et al., 2023; Micheli et al., 2023; Hafner et al., 2023; Hansen et al., 2024; Hafner et al., 2025) to large-scale video generators (Bruce et al., 2024; Peng et al., 2025; Wan et al., 2025; Wiedemer et al., 2025). Recent approaches (Zhou et al., 2025b; Bar et al., 2025; Assran et al., 2025; Goswami et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025b) leverage pretrained vision-only or vision-language representations for planning, but still require in-domain trajectories. We investigate whether world modeling emerges directly from multimodal pretraining, reducing the need for domain-specific data. Scaling laws. Language model loss scales as power law with compute (Kaplan et al., 2020; Radford et al., 2018; Brown et al., 2020). Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022; Muennighoff et al., 2023) quantified compute-optimal parameter-token tradeoffs, which were later extended to MoE (Tian et al., 2026) and diffusion models (Polyak et al., 2024). We conduct Chinchilla-style IsoFLOP analysis for joint vision-language scaling, revealing an asymmetry between modalities that MoE architectures help harmonize (Section 7).

sections.title: 9 Discussion
sections.content: We set out to bring empirical clarity to the design space of unified multimodal pretraining. central question in this space is whether vision and language can coexist in single model without mutual degradation. By training from scratch and systematically studying one variable at time, we find that modality competition is not fatal flaw of multimodal pretrainingit is just symptom of specific design choices. single visual representation suffices. Previous work has assumed that separate visual representations are necessary for understanding and generation (Deng et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025). We join growing body of work (Shi et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025c; Tong et al., 2026; Zheng et al., 2026; Shi et al., 2026) that challenges the necessity of this dichotomy. Our results show that single high-dimensional semantic representation (RAE) excels at both visual understanding and generation (Section 3), and our MoE analysis confirms that the same experts are often activated for both tasks (Section 6.2). This convergence was fully learned from the data without any human priors. Furthermore, RAE representations continue to improve as capacity scales, while VAE-based methods exhibit loss saturation (Figure 17), suggesting that semantic representations are better suited as the foundation for scaling unified models. However, we note that current semantic vision encoders can still lag behind VAEs in fine-grained reconstruction (Zhang et al., 2025b). We hope our findings will encourage the community to close this gap by developing better generation-aware semantic representations. Modality competition is largely solvable. common assumption is that vision and language are inherently at odds within single model, meaning that training on one modality necessarily degrades the other (Lin et al., 2024). Our findings paint different picture: the modality tax has two identifiable sources, neither of which is the visual modality itself. The first is data: friction stems from distributional shifts in image-text captions, not from vision itself (Section 4). Pure video is complementary to language, and general multimodal pretraining yields positive transfer for VQA and world modeling (Sections 4.3 and 5). The second is architectural: dense models rigidly allocate capacity between modalities, which modality-specific FFNs partially address, and MoE further resolves by learning to allocate capacity per token (Section 6.1). We note that multimodal pretraining can slightly degrade out-of-distribution text generalization, but the in-distribution impact is minimal (Figure 5), and expect this gap to narrow with more training. Rather than viewing vision as competitor, we can leverage vast quantities of visual data, such as pure video, as strategic resource for the next generation of models. Efficient and effective scaling with MoE. MoE has proven effective for scaling language models (Liu et al., 2024a,b), but making it work for multimodal pretraining requires additional design. We find that high granularity is essential and that the choice of diffusion prediction target depends on the visual representation (Section 6.1). With these design choices, both modalities consistently improve as total experts grow at fixed active compute (Figure 16), confirming that sparse MoE scaling extends to the multimodal setting. Our IsoFLOP analysis (Section 7) reveals that MoE resolves fundamental scaling asymmetry: in dense models, language follows Chinchilla-like balanced allocation while vision is significantly more data-hungry, making it impossible to optimize both simultaneously. However, in the sparse MoE regime, language scaling shifts toward more data-hungry regime, aligning with vision scaling. This suggests that sparsity plays 20 deeper role in multimodal models than efficiency aloneit provides the structural flexibility for enabling modalities with fundamentally different scaling behaviors to coexist. Whats next? The finding that world modeling capabilities emerge from general multimodal pretraining with minimal domain-specific data (Section 5), aligns with the vision that general-purpose models, trained broadly on how the world looks and moves, can develop an internal model of physical reality (LeCun, 2022). The ingredients are already available; vast quantities of unlabeled video remain largely untapped, and our results show they can be incorporated without degrading language capabilities. As unified models grow in capacity and data, we believe the boundary between multimodal models and world models will blur, and future models will evolve into truly native, end-to-end multimodal systems. This work offers clearer understanding of the core challenges in unified multimodal pretraining, and we hope that our insights will encourage the community to extend the foundation model paradigm beyond just LLMs, towards systems that can understand and reason about the physical world (LeCun, 2022).
