sections.title: Appendix
sections.content: This appendix provides supplementary analyses and implementation details supporting the main paper: discusses limitations of our work and directions for future research. provides detailed explanations of unified multimodal pretraining mechanics, including sequence formatting, hybrid attention masking, joint flow matching, and the modal-switching inference algorithm. compares shared vs. modality-specific FFN architectures for cross-modal learning. showcases more explorations: model properties in different layers, an adaptive loss centering mechanism, and whether vision is complementary to language modeling. provides architecture, pretraining, inference, vision encoder, data composition, VQA evaluation, and MoE implementation details. provides world modeling implementation and evaluation details, and more qualitative examples of zero-shot rollouts and counterfactual simulation with free-form natural language actions. describes training data sources, recaptioning procedures, video action annotations, and additional data analyses.

sections.title: A Limitations and Future Work
sections.content: We focus on investigating multimodal pretraining from scratch rather than post-training. Unified multimodal models can improve greatly through RL or even multimodal RL by enabling the system to both generate and interpret visual latents. On the architecture front, while we explore many facets of multimodal Mixtureof-Experts (MoE) design, our scope is not exhaustive; for instance, the uneven distribution of tokens per expert remains significant bottleneck for hardware utilization. Conversely, leveraging higher granularity may enhance the scaling efficiency of these MoEs. Regarding data, our work excludes interleaved data, which remains critical area for future exploration. In the long run, we believe the capability to spontaneously generate visual latents will drive the convergence of multimodal models and world modeling, fostering "System 2" behaviors that are grounded in the real world. Extended Methodology: Unified Multimodal Mechanics We provide more detailed explanations of our unified pretraining and inference mechanics. Building upon the Transfusion framework, our model processes discrete text tokens and continuous visual inputs within single, shared transformer backbone. Sequence formatting. Text tokens are mapped to vectors via standard nn.Embedding layer. Visual inputs are mapped to latent patches using frozen visual encoder and flattened into 1D sequences. Because videos are encoded frame-by-frame, we enclose each individual image or video frame with beginning-of-image (<BOI>) and end-of-image (<EOI>) marker tokens. Consequently, video is processed as series of individually bounded frames within the sequence (e.g. <BOI> frame 1 <EOI> <BOI> frame 2 <EOI>). Hybrid attention masking. To process this mixed sequence, we implement hybrid attention mask using FlexAttention. Text tokens apply standard causal mask, attending only to preceding elements in the sequence. For visual data, we apply block-wise causal mask: patches within the exact same image (or video frame) attend to one another bidirectionally, but can only attend causally to text or visual frames that appeared previously in the sequence. This ensures the model captures full spatial context within single frame while preserving the strict autoregressive structure of the overall multimodal sequence. Joint training and flow matching. During training, we apply distinct objectives to each modality within the mixed sequence. For text, the input tokens remain discrete, and the model is trained using standard next-token prediction. For visual data, we employ frame-wise flow matching. Given clean sequence of visual latent tokens z0, we sample an independent timestep U[0, 1] for the entire image or video frame. We inject Gaussian noise to create the interpolated noisy latents zt, which are then fed into the transformer alongside the text tokens. Consequently, any text tokens appearing after an image in the sequence natively condition on these noisy visual representations. The transformer then simultaneously predicts the next discrete text tokens and the continuous velocity field vθ required to denoise zt back to z0. Loss computation and routing. The model optimizes the joint objective = λLM LLM + λf lowLf low in single forward-backward pass. The transformers output hidden states are routed to modality-specific heads based on their sequence position. States corresponding to discrete text project to vocabulary head to compute the cross-entropy loss LLM per token. Conversely, tokens within the <BOI> and <EOI> boundaries project to continuous linear head to predict the velocity field, computing the flow matching loss Lf low over the entire visual frame. Modal-switching inference algorithm. At inference, the model dynamically transitions between discrete autoregressive generation and continuous flow matching. In text mode, the model samples discrete tokens autoregressively. Upon sampling <BOI> token, the decoding algorithm suspends text generation and appends sequence of pure noise vectors (e.g. 256 tokens for 16 16 grid) to the context. The model then denoises these patches using 25-step Euler sampler, predicting the velocity field and updating the continuous tokens in place at each step. Once the visual frame is fully generated, an <EOI> token is appended, and the model resumes standard text generation. Shared vs. Modality-Specific FFNs Recent work demonstrates that modality separation improves cross-modal learning, but differs in the degree of architectural decoupling (Lin et al., 2024; Liang et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2024; Deng et al., 2025). For example, some works use modality-specific FFNs, MoE with fixed experts per modality, and/or modality-specific attention blocks. We study the simplest instantiation of architectural decoupling: shared self-attention with separate vision-only and language-only FFNs (modality-specific FFN). We compare this to model that instead uses shared FFN per layer for all tokens (shared FFN), but is otherwise identical. While modality-specific FFN increases total parameter count, training and inference FLOPs remain constant as only one FFN is activated per token. We conduct our comparison at scale of 1T tokens (520B text + 520B multimodal) to ensure that both models are well-trained. Figure 3 demonstrates that modality-specific FFN reduces text perplexity while simultaneously improving performance in image generation and visual understanding, compared to shared FFN. Consequently, we adopt the modality-specific FFN architecture as the default setting, unless otherwise specified. In Section 6.1, we further extend the idea of modality separation by exploring MoE architectures.

sections.title: D Additional Results and Exploration
sections.content: D.1 Layer-wise Performance Recent work shows that visual features exhibit different properties throughout different layers of the vision encoder (Bolya et al., 2025). We use layer-wise linear probing to study two questions: (1) how semantic understanding and low-level pixel fidelity evolve across layers of the SigLIP 2 encoder, and (2) whether the transformer backbone preserves the encoders semantic representations after training. For each layer, we train linear classifier on frozen features and report ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) accuracy to measure semantic understanding, and PSNR to measure pixel-level reconstruction quality. Quantitative results are shown in Figures 28a and 28b. Representation encoder. Across SigLIP 2 layers, ImageNet accuracy rises steadily from 12.8% (layer 0) to 86.3% (layer 26), while PSNR drops from 29.6 dB to 20.9 dB. This reveals fundamental trade-off in which deeper encoder layers progressively discard fine-grained spatial information in favor of increasingly abstract semantic features. In practice, our models train on the last-layer features of SigLIP 2, which maximizes semantic content but leaves improvements in generation quality on the table due to the degraded pixel fidelity at that depth. Training on features from multiple layers could in principle recover this fidelity, but at the cost of simplicity. This underscores the need for future vision encoders that achieve better balance between semantic abstraction and spatial fidelity within single representation. As such, developing generation-aware features represents promising direction for future research. We show qualitative examples in Figure 27. Finally, we note that these observations are conditioned on the specific MetaCLIP training mixture used to 31 Figure 27 Qualitative reconstruction results across various SigLIP 2 layers using RAE. Observations indicate that earlier layers yield higher-fidelity reconstructions. We trained our own decoder over 3M images for this ablation. train the decoder. Recent studies (Tong et al., 2026) indicate that scaling the data volume and targeting specific data distributions when training the decoder, can mitigate this degradation in fidelity. Transformer backbone. We evaluate the visual representations within the network using ImageNet-1K linear probing. Despite the complexity of the joint training mixturewhich spans language modeling, video prediction, text-to-image, image-to-text, and action-conditioned predictionthe learned transformer preserves the semantic quality of the features. Specifically, the linear probe accuracy of the backbone representations slightly surpasses that of the frozen SigLIP input, exhibiting minor improvements across successive layers. This indicates that high-quality visual representations are preserved within the learned model. We hypothesize that this retention is driven by two complementary training dynamics: the image-to-text (I2T) objective functions as caption-based representation learning (Tschannen et al., 2024; Wan et al., 2024), while the continuous diffusion objective implicitly learns rich semantic representations (Li et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2025). D.2 Loss Centering Here, we study additional optimization techniques in multimodal training. In the standard Transfusion framework, the joint loss = λLMLLM + λflowLflow uses fixed scalar weights throughout training (e.g. λLM=1, λflow=3). However, different visual encoders produce flow matching losses of vastly different magnitudes. When the vision loss is large relative to the text loss, fixed weighting scheme might over-emphasize the visual objective, degrading language performance. Conversely, when the vision loss is small, the model under-invests in generation quality. single fixed ratio cannot accommodate this variation across encoders nor across training stages as losses evolve. Inspired by the centering mechanism in DINO (Caron et al., 2021), we replace the fixed loss weights Method. with adaptive, per-step weights derived from an exponential moving average (EMA) of the loss magnitudes. At each training step, we compute weighted center of the current losses: ccurrent = α Lflow + (1 α) LLM, (5) 32 (a) SigLIP 2 encoder (b) Transformer backbone Figure 28 Layer-wise linear probing results. (a) ImageNet accuracy and PSNR across SigLIP 2 encoder layers reveal an inverse relationship between semantic understanding and pixel fidelity. (b) The Transformer backbone maintains high ImageNet accuracy throughout all layers; the dotted line marks SigLIP 2s last-layer accuracy for reference (86.3%). where α [0, 1] controls the desired emphasis between vision and text. The running center is updated via EMA: ctarget µ ctarget + (1 µ) ccurrent, with momentum µ. The per-modality weights are then: wflow = ctarget Lflow , wLM = ctarget LLM . (6) This normalizes each loss to the shared center, automatically up-weighting the smaller loss and down-weighting the larger one. The parameter α provides tunable knob: setting α closer to 1 emphasizes vision, while α closer to 0 emphasizes text. Results. Table 3 compares models trained with fixed weights (Uncentered) against loss centering (Centered) across three visual encoders. Loss centering consistently improves DPG score across all encoders, with SigLIP 2 obtaining the largest gain (+0.042). The trade-off is modest increase in text perplexity, with SigLIP 2 incurring the largest penalty (+0.30). Notably, even after centering, SigLIP 2 retains the best DPG score (0.612) while its perplexity remains competitive. The α parameter offers practitioners direct control over this trade-off, enabling task-specific tuning without architecture changes. Table 3 Effect of loss centering on generation quality (DPG ) and text perplexity (PPL ) across visual encoders. Centering consistently improves DPG at modest PPL cost. DPG Score () Perplexity () Encoder Uncentered Centered Uncentered Centered SigLIP 2 FLUX.1 SD-VAE 0.570 0.498 0.470 0.612 (+.042) 0.510 (+.012) 0.480 (+.010) 15.06 14.96 15.13 15.36 (+.30) 15.14 (+.18) 15.35 (+.22) D.3 Text Performance on Data Composition Studies Vision and language are complementary. We measure how each modality affects the other by training models across all combinations of {0, 25, 50, 75, 100}B text and multimodal tokens (Figure 29). Consistent with Section 4.1, adding visual tokens to fixed text budget minimally impacts text perplexity with small improvements on DCLM and slight degradation on Notes. Adding text tokens to fixed multimodal budget consistently improves performance, clearly surpassing the vision-only baseline. 33 Figure 29 Multimodal co-training exceeds unimodal performance. We compare multimodal models (solid lines) against unimodal baselines (dashed lines) across varying budgets.

sections.title: E Implementation Details
sections.content: E.1 Architecture Details Unlike Transfusion (Zhou et al., 2025a), we use simple linear projection layers instead of U-Net for visual input/output projections. Our architecture is encoder-agnostic and accepts any frozen visual encoder. For the loss weighting = λLMLLM + λflowLflow, we use λLM=1.0 and λflow=3.0 by default (Transfusion uses λLM=1.0 and λflow=6.0). E.2 Pretraining Details By default, training uses sequence length of 4096, 128 GPUs, and batch size of 4 per GPU, for total batch size of 2M tokens per step. The total number of training steps is calculated according to the total 4, 1000 warmup steps, and cosine decay training budget. We use AdamW optimizer with peak LR of 310 to 5% of the peak LR. E.3 Inference Details Text generation uses autoregressive next-token prediction. Visual generation denoises via 25-step Euler sampler with classifier-free guidance scale of 3.0 and 10% conditioning dropout during training (Ho & Salimans, 2022). For VAE encoders, we use pretrained decoders from SD-VAE (Rombach et al., 2022) and FLUX.1 (Labs, 2024). For SigLIP 2 So400m, DINOv2-L, and WebSSL-L, we use the RAE decoder (Zheng et al., 2026). E.4 Vision Encoders The model receives 256 tokens per image for all vision encoder types, as summarized in Table 4. Raw pixel and RAE encoders (SigLIP 2, DINOv2, WebSSL) natively produce 1616 = 256 tokens using patch size 1414 on 224224 images. For VAE encoders (SD-VAE, FLUX.1 VAE) that natively produce 3232 = 1024 latent patches, we apply PixelUnshuffle(2) (Shi et al., 2016) to downsample the spatial grid by increasing channel dimension, reducing to 1616 = 256 tokens while increasing the channel dimension by 4. This keeps the total number of vision tokens consistent for fair comparison across vision encoders. To apply the flow matching loss, we then use PixelShuffle(2) to upsample the output of the Transformer back to the original resolution of the vision encoder output. We opt for simplicity instead of using U-Net which would introduce additional parameters. 34 Table 4 Vision encoder configurations. All encoders produce 256 tokens (1616 grid) per image. For VAE encoders that natively produce 3232 = 1024 latent patches, we apply PixelUnshuffle(2) (Shi et al., 2016) to rearrange the spatial grid into channels, reducing to 1616 = 256 tokens while increasing the channel dimension by 4. To apply the diffusion loss, we then apply PixelShuffle(2) to upsample the feature map back to the original spatial resolution. Type Encoder Resolution Patch Size Grid Size Spatial Adaptation Tokens Raw Pixel Raw Pixel RAE VAE SigLIP 2 So400m DINOv2-L WebSSL-L SD-VAE FLUX.1 VAE 2242 2242 2242 2242 2562 2562 1414 1414 1414 1414 88 8 1616 1616 1616 1616 3232 3232 Pixel[Un]shuffle(2) Pixel[Un]shuffle(2) 256 256 256 256 256 256 E.5 Data Composition To study the effect of data composition on multimodal model performance, we train series of models varying the amount of text and image tokens seen during training. Specifically, we use 55 grid of data compositions: text tokens from DCLM at 0, 25, 50, 75, 100 billion and image tokens from SSTK at 0, 25, 50, 75, 100 billion, yielding 25 configurations (excluding the 0-0 case). For each configuration, we evaluate language capability using perplexity on two held-out text benchmarks (DCLM validation and Notes), and vision capability using diffusion loss and GenEval. To isolate the effect of co-training, we compare mixed-modality runs against single-modality baselines: text-only models (SSTK=0) and image-only models (DCLM=0). E.6 VQA Evaluation The encoder remains frozen. We finetune the multimodal model for 1 epoch on Cambrian-7M with similar 5. For recipe as Section E.2, except with 64 GPUs (global BS of 1M tokens), and peak LR of 110 text-only models, we first warm up the vision adapter on the Cambrian-Alignment dataset (since no encoder 3. We then finetune exists during pretraining) and keep all other parameters frozen, using peak LR of 210 using the same recipe as the multimodal model. We follow previous work (Tong et al., 2024a; Fan et al., 2025a; Han et al., 2026) and evaluate the finetuned model on the 16 benchmarks grouped in Tong et al. (2024a): MMBench (Liu et al., 2024d), Seed (Ge et al., 2023), MME (Fu et al., 2025), GQA (Hudson & Manning, 2019), AI2D (Hiippala et al., 2021), MathVista (Lu et al., 2023), MMMU (Yue et al., 2024), ScienceQA (Lu et al., 2022b), OCRBench (Liu et al., 2024e), TextVQA (Singh et al., 2019), ChartQA (Masry et al., 2022), DocVQA (Mathew et al., 2021), MMVP (Tong et al., 2024b), CV-Bench 2D (Tong et al., 2024a), CV-Bench 3D (Tong et al., 2024a), and RealWorldQA (xAI, 2024). While we acknowledge that the scope (Tao & Xie, 2024; Yue et al., 2025; Brown et al., 2025a; Yang et al., 2025b; Brown et al., 2025b; Zhou et al., 2026; Tao et al., 2026; Yang et al., 2026b) of visual understanding extends beyond these benchmarks, our purpose is to use VQA as an evaluation protocol, rather than to contend with other models as general-purpose VQA model; we therefore leave more evaluations for future work. E.7 MoE models Sparsity studies. To verify the efficacy of sparse scaling, we conduct experiments using fixed computational budget per forward pass. All models are trained for approximately 57B tokens (27,248 steps) using global batch size of 512 sequences (length 4096). Training is distributed across 64 NVIDIA H200 GPUs (8 nodes). The base Transformer backbone consists of 16 layers with dimension dmodel = 2048. We use Grouped Query Attention (GQA) with 32 query heads and 8 key-value heads. The FFN expansion multiplier is set to 1.5. For the MoE layers, we enforce an IsoFLOP constraint by fixing the number of active parameters. We utilize fine-grained expert dimension dexpert = 512 and router top-k = 16. We then systematically scale the total capacity by increasing the total number of experts from 32 up to 1008. Table 5 details the resulting parameter counts, demonstrating that active parameters remain constant (1.5B), while total capacity scales with the number of experts. Table 5 Hyperparameters and parameter counts for the sparsity (IsoFLOP) sweep. We scale total parameters by increasing the expert pool while keeping active computation constant. The slight increase in active params is due to the router. Configuration Total Params Active Params Dense Baseline = 32 = 64 = 128 = 256 = 512 = 1008 1.50B 2.30B 3.92B 7.14B 13.59B 26.48B 51.46B 1.50B 1.50B 1.50B 1.50B 1.51B 1.52B 1.53B E.8 Analysis on MoE models To understand how the MoE model allocates capacity across modalities, we quantify the routing preference of each expert. We perform this analysis on the = 16 model (256 experts, 1 modality-specific expert) trained with SigLIP 2 and x-prediction. Our training comprises 50% text data, 25% video data, 22.5% of image-text data, and 2.5% action data. We train for 1T tokens. Data and Routing. We utilize held-out validation set consisting of text tokens from DCLM and image tokens from CC12M. We pass this data through the frozen trained model and record the selection frequency of every expert. Let Ntext and Nimage denote the total number of tokens processed for each modality. For given expert ei, let (i) represent the number of times that expert was selected by the router (activated in the top-k set). Specialization Score. To normalize against imbalances in the validation data size or routing budget, we compute the selection rate for expert ei and modality {text, image} as: and (i) image text R(i) = (i) Nm (7) where is the number of active experts per token (in our analysis, = 16). This represents the probability that expert ei is selected for given token of modality m. We then define the Specialization Score Si as the normalized difference between the modality-specific selection rates: Si = R(i) text R(i) text R(i) + R(i) image image . (8) The score Si ranges from 1 to +1. score of +1 indicates the expert is exclusively selected by text tokens, while 1 indicates the expert is exclusively selected by image tokens. Classification Categories. Based on the specialization score Si, we classify each expert into one of three categories: Text Expert (Si > 0.5): The expert is significantly biased toward text. score greater than 0.5 implies Rtext > 3Rimage. Vision Expert (Si < 0.5): The expert is significantly biased toward vision. score less than -0.5 implies Rimage > 3Rtext. Multimodal Expert (0.5 Si 0.5): The expert processes balanced mix of both modalities, with neither modality exceeding the selection rate of the other by factor of 3. 36 Visual understanding and generation experts. To examine whether vision experts specialize for image generation versus image understanding, we compare expert selection patterns between the two modalities using our MoE model with RAE (SigLIP 2). For image generation (text-to-image), we process images through the diffusion denoising pipeline conditioned on text prompts, recording expert selections for image tokens. For image understanding (image-to-text), we process images through the vision encoder and record expert selections during caption generation. For each expert in each layer, we compute the selection rate as the fraction of tokens from each task that are routed to that expert. We then measure the Pearson correlation between generation selection rates and understanding selection rates across all experts within each layer. We observe strong positive correlations between expert usage for image generation and understanding across all layers. Scatter plots of expert rates show points clustered along the diagonal, indicating that experts selected frequently for generation are also selected frequently for understanding. This suggests that the MoE routing learns shared vision experts that process visual information regardless of whether the task is generative or discriminative.

sections.title: F World Modeling
sections.content: F.1 Implementation Details CEM planning. We implement the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) following Navigation World Models (NWM) (Bar et al., 2025). The planner uses an 8-step horizon (2 seconds), samples =120 candidate action sequences from Gaussian distribution, and predicts 8 future frames per sequence. Trajectories are scored via LPIPS distance (Zhang et al., 2018) between the final predicted frame and the goal image. We select the top K=5 trajectories and average their actions for the final plan. Each evaluation instance repeats this process 3 times; we report Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE) against the ground truth trajectories, following NWM. Data ratio studies. We vary the proportion of action-conditioned navigation data in the training mixture. We use our default architecture with modality-specific FFN and SigLIP 2, trained for 200B tokens on 128 H100 GPUs. The base mixture allocates 25% text (DCLM), 25% video (SSv2, Kinetics, HowTo100M, YT-Temporal), and 50% multimodal. We ablate NWM data from 0.1% to 25% of total budget, with remainder split between MetaCLIP and text-action annotations. Navigation evaluation uses the RECON dataset with the CEM planner described above. WASD control definition. We map standard keyboard controls to the robot-centric coordinate system used in NWM training (where +x points forward, +y points left, and yaw is measured counter-clockwise). Each key triggers normalized displacement tuple (x, y, yaw). We define Forward (W) and Backward (S) as pure translations of (+0.5, 0, 0) and (0.5, 0, 0), respectively. To simulate realistic motion while turning, the lateral controls include small forward velocity component: Turn Left (A) maps to (+0.2, 0, +0.5) and Turn Right (D) maps to (+0.2, 0, 0.5). Free-form natural language actions. To complement the visualizations with keyboard commands, we also demonstrate the zero-shot ability to use free-form natural language as action conditioning for navigation. For example, go on the road or take big steps forward. This enables the usage of nearly infinite set of actions, going beyond mapping to pre-defined set of actions, which is limitation of recent works such as Genie 3 (Bruce et al., 2024; Parker-Holder et al., 2024; Parker-Holder & Fruchter, 2025). F.2 More Navigation Examples We showcase more navigation rollouts with either WASD controls (Forward , Left , Backward , Right ) or free-form natural language as actions, in Figure 30, Figure 31, Figure 32, Figure 33, and Figure 34. We also include examples in Figure 35 of counterfactual trajectories generated from the same context but with different commands. 37 Figure 30 More navigation examples with natural language commands (I). sections.title: G Data
sections.content: G.1 Data Sources We present the detailed data sources in Table 6. G.2 MetaCLIP Recaption To improve caption quality, we recaption images using Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct (Bai et al., 2025). We deploy the model via vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023) with 8-way tensor parallelism per node, enabling efficient batch inference at scale. We use nucleus sampling with temperature 0.6 and top-p 0.95, generating up to 128 tokens per caption. The system prompt used for recaptioning is shown below: Recaptioning Prompt You are an expert image captioner. Write one specific, information-dense caption (12 sentences). Do not begin with A, An, or The. Name the main subject first, then key objects, attributes, actions, and scene context. Avoid hedging and meta phrases. 38 Figure 31 More navigation examples with natural language commands (II). G.3 Video Action Annotations We collected about 12M video + text action annotations from YouTube-Temporal 1B (Zellers et al., 2022) using model-based pipeline, where MLLM is asked to caption the transition between two consecutive video chunks, with focus on describing physical actions rather than just changes in appearance. The resulting annotations are then verified via self-reflection where the model is asked both whether the caption describes an action, and whether the action is reflective of the video. For each sequence, 1-4 context frames are provided, along with text action, and 1-4 resulting frames. Varying the number of context and resulting frames helps prevent model collapse to trivial solutions and improves robustness to video length. We leverage this data to supplement the action trajectories from NWM. G.4 Cosine Distance Computation To measure the distributional similarity between DCLM and image-text datasets, we sample 100K samples from each dataset. Because some samples are long, we truncate at 2000 characters. We then extract text embedding per sample using Qwen3-Embedding-8B (Zhang et al., 2025c). Finally, we compute the cosine distance between the centroid of each dataset. 39 Figure 32 More navigation examples with natural language commands (III).
